title: Public Finance
summary: The rules that govern how public money is raised, spent, and audited.

articles.title: Principles of public finance
paragraphs: The following principles shall guide all aspects of public finance in the Republic—
(a) there shall be openness and accountability, including public participation in financial matters;
(b) the public finance system shall promote an equitable society, and in particular—
(i) the burden of taxation shall be shared fairly;
(ii) revenue raised nationally shall be shared equitably among national and county governments; and
(iii) expenditure shall promote the equitable development of the country, including by making special provision for marginalised groups and areas;
(c) the burdens and benefits of the use of resources and public borrowing shall be shared equitably between present and future generations;
(d) public money shall be used in a prudent and responsible way; and
(e) financial management shall be responsible, and fiscal reporting shall be clear.
articles.summary: Public money must be managed openly, fairly, prudently and with accountability to both current and future generations.
articles.explanation: This is the backbone of every AuditGava chart. When a county spends less than 40% of its development budget or piles up pending bills, it is Article 201 that is being broken.

articles.title: Equitable sharing of national revenue
paragraphs: (1) Revenue raised nationally shall be shared equitably among the national and county governments.
(2) County governments may be given additional allocations from the national government's share of the revenue, either conditionally or unconditionally.
articles.summary: Taxes collected at national level must be shared fairly between Nairobi and the 47 counties.
articles.explanation: The 'equitable share' you see in every county dashboard comes from this article.

articles.title: Equitable share and other financial laws
paragraphs: (1) The following criteria shall be taken into account in determining the equitable shares provided for under Article 202 and in all national legislation concerning county government enacted in terms of this Chapter—
(a) the national interest;
(b) any provision that must be made in respect of the public debt and other national obligations;
(c) the needs of the national government, determined by objective criteria;
(d) the need to ensure that county governments are able to perform the functions allocated to them;
(e) the fiscal capacity and efficiency of county governments;
(f) developmental and other needs of counties;
(g) economic disparities within and among counties and the need to remedy them;
(h) the need for affirmative action in respect of disadvantaged areas and groups;
(i) the need for economic optimisation of each county and to provide incentives for each county to optimise its capacity to raise revenue;
(j) the desirability of stable and predictable allocations of revenue; and
(k) the need for flexibility in responding to emergencies and other temporary needs.

(2) For every financial year, the equitable share of the revenue raised nationally that is allocated to county governments shall be not less than fifteen per cent of all revenue collected by the national government.
articles.summary: County allocations must consider national interest, public debt, need, fairness and economic realities — and must not fall below 15% of national revenue.

articles.title: Equalisation Fund
paragraphs: (1) There is established an Equalisation Fund into which shall be paid one half per cent of all the revenue collected by the national government each year calculated on the basis of the most recent audited accounts of revenue received, as approved by the National Assembly.

(2) The national government shall use the Equalisation Fund only to provide basic services including water, roads, health facilities and electricity to marginalised areas to the extent necessary to bring the quality of those services in those areas to the level generally enjoyed by the rest of the nation, so far as possible.
articles.summary: 0.5% of national revenue is set aside each year to bring basic services in marginalised areas up to the national average.

articles.title: Consolidated Fund and other public funds
paragraphs: (1) There is established the Consolidated Fund into which shall be paid all money raised or received by or on behalf of the national government, except money that—
(a) is reasonably excluded from the Fund by an Act of Parliament and payable into another public fund established for a specific purpose; or
(b) may, under an Act of Parliament, be retained by the State organ that received it for the purpose of defraying the expenses of the State organ.
(2) Money shall not be withdrawn from the Consolidated Fund except—
(a) in accordance with an appropriation by an Act of Parliament;
(b) as a charge against the Fund as authorised by this Constitution or an Act of Parliament; or
(c) as otherwise authorised by this Constitution.
articles.summary: All national revenue flows into one Consolidated Fund and cannot be spent without an Act of Parliament.

articles.title: Imposition of tax
paragraphs: (1) No tax or licensing fee may be imposed, waived or varied except as provided by legislation.
(2) If legislation permits the waiver of any tax or licensing fee—
(a) a public record of each waiver shall be maintained together with the reason for the waiver; and
(b) each waiver, and the reason for it, shall be reported to the Auditor-General.
(3) No law may exclude or authorise the exclusion of a State officer from payment of tax by reason of—
(a) the office held by that State officer; or
(b) the nature of the work of the State officer.
articles.summary: Taxes can only be imposed or waived by law, every waiver is public, and no state officer is exempt from paying tax.

articles.title: Public debt
paragraphs: (1) The public debt is a charge on the Consolidated Fund, but an Act of Parliament may provide for charging all or part of the public debt to other public funds.
(2) For the purposes of this Article, 'the public debt' means all financial obligations attendant to loans raised or guaranteed and securities issued or guaranteed by the national government.
articles.summary: Kenya's national debt is paid out of the Consolidated Fund unless Parliament provides otherwise.

articles.title: Form, content and timing of budgets
paragraphs: (1) Budgets of the national and county governments shall contain—
(a) estimates of revenue and expenditure, differentiating between recurrent and development expenditure;
(b) proposals for financing any anticipated deficit for the period to which they apply; and
(c) proposals regarding borrowing and other forms of public liability that will increase public debt during the following year.
(2) National legislation shall prescribe—
(a) the structure of the development plans and budgets of counties;
(b) when the plans and budgets of the counties shall be tabled in the county assemblies; and
(c) the form and manner of consultation between the national government and county governments in the process of preparing plans and budgets.
articles.summary: Every budget — national or county — must show recurrent vs. development spending, deficits and borrowing, and follow a prescribed structure and timeline.
articles.explanation: This is why AuditGava can cleanly split a county's spending into recurrent vs. development and track borrowing year over year.

articles.title: Financial control
paragraphs: (1) An Act of Parliament shall provide for the prudent and responsible management and use of public money.
(2) The Act required by clause (1) shall provide for the establishment of offices, persons or authorities with responsibility for ensuring prudent and responsible use of public money.

(3) Subject to clause (4), if the Cabinet Secretary responsible for finance is of the opinion that a County government is in material breach of measures established under the Act in clause (1) the Cabinet Secretary may stop the transfer of funds to the County government.
(4) A decision to stop the transfer of funds may not stop the transfer of more than fifty per cent of the funds due to a County government.
(5) A decision to stop the transfer of funds lapses at the end of sixty days, but may be renewed.
articles.summary: A law backs up how public money must be managed, and the National Treasury can suspend up to half of a county's transfers if it is in material breach — but only for sixty days at a time.

articles.title: Accounts and audit of public entities
paragraphs: (1) An Act of Parliament shall provide for—

(a) the keeping of financial records and the auditing of accounts of all governments and other public entities, and prescribe other measures for securing efficient and transparent fiscal management; and
(b) the designation of an accounting officer in every public entity at the national and county level of government.
(2) The accounts of all governments and State organs shall be audited by the Auditor-General.

(3) The accounting officer of a national public entity is accountable to the National Assembly for its financial management, and the accounting officer of a county public entity is accountable to the county assembly for its financial management.
(4) The Controller of Budget, in consultation with the Auditor-General, shall develop uniform norms and standards for the accounts of public entities.
articles.summary: Every public entity must keep audited accounts, and the Auditor-General is the ultimate auditor for all of them.

articles.title: Procurement of public goods and services
paragraphs: (1) When a State organ or any other public entity contracts for goods or services, it shall do so in accordance with a system that is fair, equitable, transparent, competitive and cost-effective.
(2) An Act of Parliament shall prescribe a framework within which policies relating to procurement and asset disposal shall be implemented and may provide for all or any of the following—
(a) categories of preference in the allocation of contracts;
(b) the protection or advancement of persons, categories of persons or groups previously disadvantaged by unfair competition or discrimination;
(c) sanctions against contractors that have not performed according to professionally regulated procedures, contractual agreements or legislation; and
(d) sanctions against persons who have defaulted on their tax obligations, or have been guilty of corrupt practices or serious violations of fair-employment laws.
articles.summary: All public procurement must be fair, equitable, transparent, competitive and cost-effective — and contractors who break the rules can be sanctioned.

articles.title: Controller of Budget
paragraphs: (1) There shall be a Controller of Budget who shall be nominated by the President and, with the approval of the National Assembly, appointed by the President.
(2) To be qualified to be the Controller of Budget, a person shall have extensive knowledge of public finance or at least ten years' experience in auditing public finance management.
(3) The Controller of Budget shall hold office, subject to Article 251, for a non-renewable term of eight years.
(4) The Controller of Budget shall oversee the implementation of the budgets of the national and county governments by authorising withdrawals from public funds under Articles 204, 206 and 207.
(5) The Controller of Budget shall not approve any withdrawal from a public fund unless satisfied that the withdrawal is authorised by law.
(6) Every four months, the Controller of Budget shall submit to each House of Parliament a report on the implementation of the budgets of the national and county governments.
articles.summary: The Controller of Budget is the gatekeeper — no shilling leaves a public fund without their approval, and they report to Parliament every four months.
articles.explanation: The quarterly 'County Budget Implementation Review Reports' that AuditGava uses as primary sources are published under this article.

articles.title: Auditor-General
paragraphs: (1) There shall be an Auditor-General who shall be nominated by the President and, with the approval of the National Assembly, appointed by the President.
(2) To be qualified to be the Auditor-General, a person shall have extensive knowledge of public finance or at least ten years' experience in auditing or public finance management.
(3) The Auditor-General shall hold office, subject to Article 251, for a term of eight years and shall not be eligible for re-appointment.
(4) Within six months after the end of each financial year, the Auditor-General shall audit and report, in respect of that financial year, on—
(a) the accounts of the national and county governments;
(b) the accounts of all funds and authorities of the national and county governments;
(c) the accounts of all courts;
(d) the accounts of every commission and independent office established by this Constitution;
(e) the accounts of the National Assembly, the Senate and the county assemblies;
(f) the accounts of political parties funded from public funds;
(g) the public debt; and
(h) the accounts of any other entity that legislation requires the Auditor-General to audit.
(5) The Auditor-General may audit and report on the accounts of any entity that is funded from public funds.
(6) An audit report shall confirm whether or not public money has been applied lawfully and in an effective way.
(7) Audit reports shall be submitted to Parliament or the relevant county assembly within three months after the audit.
(8) Within three months after receiving an audit report, Parliament or the county assembly shall debate and consider the report and take appropriate action.
articles.summary: The Auditor-General audits every shilling of public money within six months of year end, and Parliament or the county assembly must act on each report within three months.
articles.explanation: This is the single article that makes AuditGava possible. The entire "Audit Findings" tab, the flagged amounts, and the accountability grade exist because Article 229 guarantees every county is audited and those reports are public.

articles.title: Salaries and Remuneration Commission
paragraphs: (1) There is established the Salaries and Remuneration Commission.
(2) The Commission shall consist of the following persons appointed by the President—
(a) a chairperson;
(b) one person each nominated by the following bodies from among persons who are not public servants—
(i) the Parliamentary Service Commission;
(ii) the Public Service Commission;
(iii) the Judicial Service Commission;
(iv) the Teachers Service Commission;
(v) the National Police Service Commission;
(vi) the Defence Council; and
(vii) the Senate, on behalf of the county governments;
(c) two persons, one man and one woman, to represent trade unions, nominated by the most representative organisation of trade unions;
(d) two persons, one man and one woman, nominated by an umbrella body representing employers; and
(e) one person nominated by the joint forum of professional bodies as provided by legislation.
(3) The Commission shall—
(a) set and regularly review the remuneration and benefits of all State officers; and
(b) advise the national and county governments on the remuneration and benefits of all other public officers.
(4) In performing its functions, the Commission shall take the following principles into account—
(a) the need to ensure that the total public compensation bill is fiscally sustainable;
(b) the need to ensure that the public services are able to attract and retain the skills required to execute their functions;
(c) the need to recognise productivity and performance; and
(d) transparency and fairness.
articles.summary: An independent commission sets and reviews the pay of every state officer and keeps the public wage bill sustainable.
